Examples

  • Gallery

eqX

eqX equal-splits a set of panels across the horizontal range: it distributes the distinct keys of an index column evenly and emits an x (panel left) and w (panel width, the step) for each. It is the simplest subplot / facet driver — the same rank→step shape as the bandX scale, promoted to a layout. Outputs are normalized [0, 1]; the frame affine turns them into pixels. Its y-mirror is eqY (row facets); pick the orientation by which operator you reach for (or, historically, by which channels you .pick() onto).

const v = canvas(720, 320);
v.subplot().dot('penguins', { x: linear('bill_length_mm'),    y: linear('body_mass_g') });
v.subplot().dot('penguins', { x: linear('flipper_length_mm'), y: linear('body_mass_g') });
v.subplots({ ...eqX('i').pick('x', 'w') });   // 'i' = the panel index (a virtual table)
return v;

Two side-by-side panels sharing a single y-axis. v.subplot() appends one row { i: k } to a virtual panel-index table; eqX('i') splits over that index; the panel widths are solved from label demand so the plot areas line up.

Options

eqX(key) takes a single input — the index/key column to split over. It fits a 1-row broadcast domain (a countDistinct of the key) and emits equal shares: x = rank(key) / n, w = 1 / n. The fit is a bare count, so the domain is unshareable and unticked (two panel counts do not union into a shared domain — there is no axis for it).

Input Type Meaning
key unknown the index column whose distinct values become panels ('i', 'species', …)

.pick() outputs:

Output Space Meaning
x relative coord x panel left edge = rank(key) / n
w relative len x panel width = 1 / n (the step)

Bind by spreading into a channel object: ...eqX('species').pick('x', 'w'). eqX takes no options bag — spacing is exactly equal (use bandX if you want inner/outer padding).

Examples

Two subplots over the panel index — the canonical form. v.subplots({...}) binds the container's box channels from the virtual i table:

const v = canvas(720, 320);
v.subplot().dot('penguins', { x: linear('bill_length_mm'),    y: linear('body_mass_g') });
v.subplot().dot('penguins', { x: linear('flipper_length_mm'), y: linear('body_mass_g') });
v.subplots({ ...eqX('i').pick('x', 'w') });
return v;

Data-driven small multiples — one panel per row of a host table. The species rect table is the container; eqX('species') lays out its panels; the nested dot layer is keyed on species:

const species = db.table('penguins').groupby(['species'], {});  // one row per species (distinct)
const v = canvas(760, 260);
v.rect(species, { ...eqX('species').pick('x', 'w'), y: 12, h: 236, fill: '#f5f6fa' })
 .dot('penguins',
   { x: linear('bill_length_mm'), y: linear('body_mass_g'), fill: '#4e79a7' },
   { on: eq('species', 'species') });   // child.species = host.species
return v;

Free (per-panel) scales — shared: false trains a domain per panel so each panel's axis spans its own species' range and the points fill every panel:

const species = db.table('penguins').groupby(['species'], {});
const v = canvas(760, 260);
v.rect(species, { ...eqX('species').pick('x', 'w'), y: 12, h: 236, fill: '#f5f6fa' })
 .dot('penguins',
   { x: linear('bill_length_mm', { shared: false }),
     y: linear('body_mass_g',    { shared: false }), fill: '#59a14f' },
   { on: eq('species', 'species') });
return v;

The general nesting mechanism — the container rect is itself encoded (an ordinal fill), and nest() opens the template explicitly so you can add as many keyed layers as you like:

const species = db.table('penguins').groupby(['species'], {});
const v = canvas(760, 260);
const cell = v.rect(species,
    { ...eqX('species').pick('x', 'w'), y: 12, h: 236, fill: ordinal('species') })
  .nest('penguins', { on: eq('species', 'species') });
cell.dot({ x: linear('bill_length_mm'), y: linear('body_mass_g'), fill: '#ffffff' });
return v;

The partition model — eqX lays out one rect per distinct community, and a nested force runs per panel. eqX supplies only the panel x/w; the panel's y/h are pixel literals:

const communities = db.table('members').groupby(['community'], {});  // distinct communities
db.declareFK({ fromTable: 'members', fromCols: ['community'],
               toTable: communities.name, toCols: ['community'] });
const v = canvas(760, 360);
const panel = v.rect(communities, {
  ...eqX('community').pick('x', 'w'), y: 12, h: 330, fill: '#f5f6fa',
});
panel.nest('members', { on: eq('community', 'community') }).dot({
  ...force('follows').pick('x', 'y'),
  r: 6, fill: ordinal('community'),
});
return v;

No layout at all — for the record, eqX is what you'd reach for to force a row, but you often don't need it: two panels that share a scale let the solver infer the row on its own:

const v = canvas(720, 320);
v.subplot().dot('penguins', { x: linear('bill_length_mm'),    y: linear('body_mass_g') });
v.subplot().dot('penguins', { x: linear('flipper_length_mm'), y: linear('body_mass_g') });
// no v.subplots(...): the shared y makes the solver place the panels side by side
return v;

See also

  • eqY — the y-mirror; distinct keys → equal vertical split (row facets)
  • bandX — the same rank→step shape as a trained categorical scale (with padding options)
  • proportionalX — panels sized by a value instead of equally
  • treemap — 2D space partition when one row of panels isn't enough
  • force — composes with eqX panels via .nest(...) for a per-panel simulation
